title: Valid Sudoku Backtracking Bug Fix: Unnecessary Backtrack

statement.markdown: Given a 9x9 Sudoku board, determine if it is possible to place the numbers 1-9 in the empty cells so that each row, column, and 3x3 sub-grid contains each of the numbers exactly once. The board is represented as a 2D array, where 0 marks an empty cell. The function should return True if the board is a valid Sudoku, and False otherwise.
statement.inputFormat: The input format is a 2D array, where rows and columns are 0-indexed, and a 0 mark an empty cell.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean value indicating whether the Sudoku board is valid or not.
statement.notes: Do not modify the board's state. The empty cells are marked by 0.

constraints.description: The Sudoku board as a 2D array

examples.explanation: The Sudoku board is not valid

hints.text: Backtracking is a technique used to solve problems by recursively trying all possible solutions and abandoning them when they lead to a dead-end.

editorial.title: Editorial: Valid Sudoku Backtracking Bug Fix
editorial.intuition: The function should use backtracking to try all possible numbers in each cell and see if it leads to a valid solution.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force approach would involve trying all possible numbers in each cell and checking if they lead to a valid solution.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would use backtracking to try numbers in each cell in a systematic way.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses backtracking to try numbers in each cell in a systematic way and checks if they lead to a valid solution. It also uses a helper function to check if a number can be placed in a cell.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness involves showing that the algorithm correctly checks if a Sudoku board is valid or not.
commonMistakes: Placing a number in a cell without checking if it leads to a valid solution
